skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate and transform videos through the inference.sh CLI using hosted AI video models for text-to-video, image animation, lipsync, avatars, upscaling, and foley sound.
skill.summary: Create AI videos and media transformations through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.author: inference-shell

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, example CLI commands, and documented URLs. The real risk is that the skill is intentionally designed to run the inference.sh CLI and send prompts, media URLs, and generated assets to hosted inference services, so users need clear data and cost warnings. No prompt injection attempt, credential theft, obfuscation, or confirmed malicious behavior was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Networked AI Video CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash(infsh *) and provides infsh app run examples. This is expected for the skill, but prompts, media URLs, and generated video jobs are sent to external hosted inference services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated infsh app run examples confirm intentional external command execution. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it creates real network, privacy, and billing exposure.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Skill Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes npx skills add commands for installing related skills. These commands are not within the declared infsh-only tool allowance, but users who run them would fetch code from an external source.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples, not hidden execution. They still expand the trust boundary if copied or executed by a user.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer labeled many Markdown inline code spans and fenced bash examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md is documentation, not Ruby code, so these detections are not direct code execution by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is a Markdown skill file and the suspicious tokens appear in inline code or fenced examples. The executable infsh examples are handled separately as a medium operational risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines contain frontmatter description text and a Markdown table header. I did not find evidence of cryptographic APIs, hashes, weak algorithms, or encryption logic in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of both lines shows descriptive text and a table header only. There is no semantic evidence of cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Expected Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are the inference.sh website, a preview image, documentation links, and placeholder media inputs. I did not find evidence that secrets or local files are sent to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible user-facing references and examples. They still indicate expected network use, but not covert exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed External CLI Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed-tools header permits infsh commands. This is necessary for the skill, but marketplace users should know it can initiate remote inference jobs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and quick start command directly confirm the pattern. The risk is operational, not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Media URL Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples accept image_url, audio_url, or video_url values. These workflows may expose user media URLs to external inference services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly pass remote media URL fields. This is normal for media generation, but it has privacy and access-control implications.

content.value_statement: Video generation workflows often require switching between many hosted model tools. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise command guide for running inference.sh video apps.
actual_capabilities: Lists inference.sh video model app IDs for text-to-video, image-to-video, avatar, lipsync, upscaling, foley, and merge workflows.
Shows infsh login and infsh app run commands for common video generation tasks.
Provides examples for Veo, Grok Imagine Video, Wan, OmniHuman, Fabric, PixVerse, Topaz, and HunyuanVideo Foley.
Documents how to browse available video apps through the inference.sh CLI.
Links to inference.sh documentation for running apps, streaming results, and media pipelines.
limitations: Requires a working inference.sh CLI installation and user authentication.
Generation quality, speed, pricing, and availability depend on external hosted models.
Media URLs and prompts may be sent to third-party inference services.
The skill provides command guidance and does not include custom video editing code.

use_cases.title: Prototype Marketing Clips
use_cases.description: Generate short video concepts from product prompts, then compare model outputs for style and quality.

use_cases.title: Animate Existing Images
use_cases.description: Turn a product still, portrait, or concept image into a short motion clip using image-to-video models.

use_cases.title: Build Media Pipelines
use_cases.description: Combine generation, lipsync, foley, upscaling, and merging commands into repeatable content workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-video-generation skill to create an inference.sh command for a five second video of a calm city street at sunrise.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to choose an image-to-video model and prepare a command for animating this image URL: https://example.com/product.jpg.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Talking Avatar
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to recommend a lipsync or avatar model and prepare a command using a portrait URL and narration audio URL.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Video Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to outline commands for generating a clip, adding foley sound, upscaling it, and merging it with a second clip.

output_examples.input: Create a video from a prompt about a flower blooming.
output_examples.output: Recommended model: Veo 3.1 Fast. The response would provide a ready infsh command and note that the prompt is sent to inference.sh.

output_examples.input: Animate a product image and add subtle camera movement.
output_examples.output: Recommended model: Wan 2.5 or Wan 2.5 I2V. The response would request a usable image URL and generate the matching command.

output_examples.input: Make a talking head video from a portrait and audio file.
output_examples.output: Recommended model: OmniHuman, Fabric, or PixVerse Lipsync. The response would explain which media URLs are required before running the job.

best_practices: Confirm the inference.sh account, model pricing, and usage limits before running generation jobs.
Use non-sensitive prompts and media URLs unless the service terms allow the data being submitted.
Start with short durations and lower-cost models before running high-quality production jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private media URLs or confidential prompts without approval.
Do not run npx installation commands from documentation without verifying the package source.
Do not assume every listed model supports the same duration, resolution, audio, or input fields.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides the agent to use the inference.sh CLI, which runs hosted video models.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it support text-to-video?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for Veo, Grok Imagine Video, and Seedance text-to-video models.

faq.question: Can it animate an existing image?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes image-to-video examples using models such as Wan and Seedance Lite.

faq.question: What are the main security considerations?
faq.answer: The main concerns are external CLI execution, network submission of prompts or media URLs, and possible usage charges.

faq.question: Does the audit find malicious code?
faq.answer: No. The audit found no confirmed malicious behavior, but recommends a medium-risk warning for external service use.
